turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 17-587, Mount Lemmon Fire District v. Guido, Justice Ginsburg has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Does the Age discrimination in Employment Act apply to a state's political subdivision that employs fewer than 20 workers? That the question this case presents. Defendant petitioner is the Mount Lemmon Fire District, a political subdivision in Arizona, which laid off John Guido and Dennis Rankin its oldest full-time firefighters. Guido and Rankin sued seeking redress under the Age Discrimination Act. The fire district responded that it is not covered by the Act because its workers fall short of the Act's 20-employee threshold. United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit held that political subdivisions of a state are covered by the Age Discrimination Act without regard to the number of workers they employ. We affirm that judgment. To trace the statutory development we start with Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 which prohibits employment discrimination on the basis of race, color, religion, sex and national origin. Initially Title VII governed only private-sector employment. The same was true of the Age Discrimination Act enacted in 1967 three years after Title VII to protect workers against opportunity age discrimination. In 1972, Congress amended Title VII to reach the state and local governments. Congress did so by defining state and local governments as persons engaged in an industry affecting commerce. Title VII applies in numerical threshold, now 15-employees, to all covered persons both private and public employers. Two years after the Title VII amendment, in 1974 Congress amended the Age Discrimination Act to cover state and local governments, but the formulation of Age Discrimination Act's amendment differed markedly from Title VII's alteration. The Age Discrimination Act's definitional provision now reads the term 'employer' means a person engaged in an industry affecting commerce who has 20 or more employees. The term also means (1) any agent of such a person and (2) a state or political subdivision of the state. In the same 1974 enactment, Congress amended the Fair Labor Standards Act on which parts of the Age Discrimination Act had been modeled to reach all government employers regardless of the number of workers they employ. Does it also means a state of political subdivision of a state add a new category to the definition of employer as Guido and Rankin urge as the fire district argues does it also means merely clarify the states and their political subdivisions are among the persons encompassed by the definitional provision's persons which covers persons engaged in commerce having at least 20 employees. Federal courts have divided on the answer to that question and we now resolve the conflict. The words 'also' means, we conclude, add additional categories of employers to the Age Discrimination Act's reach newly including states and subdivisions of states. The ordinary meaning of 'also' means is additive. Those words also means appear dozens of times to add to US code typically carrying an additive meaning and we see no reason to depart from that meaning here. These and other reasons for our conclusion are detailed in the Court's opinion. True applying the Age Discrimination Act as written the states and political subdivisions regardless of size makes the Age Discrimination Act broader in this respect than Title VII, but the disparity is a consequence of the different language Congress employed. The better comparator is the Fair Labor Standards Act on which many aspects of Age Discrimination Act are based. The Fair Labor Standards Act I just mentioned ranks states and political subdivisions as employers regardless of the number of employees they have. In short, the text of the Age Discrimination Act's definitional provision also the Act's kinship to the Fair Labor Standards Act and linguistic differences from Title VII persuade us that state and local governments qualify as employers governed by the Age Discrimination Act regardless of the number of workers employed they employ. The Court's opinion affirming the Ninth Circuit's judgment is unanimous. Justice Kavanagh took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
